reasoning: The case presents a clear instance of selective enforcement where an officer explicitly ignored three identical violators to target the user. A reasonable person would immediately identify this disparity as unfair application of the law, satisfying the criteria for an application defeat.

The injustice is obvious because compliance is factually impossible due to the government's own failure (destroyed records). No reasonable person would defend a bureaucratic system that demands a non-existent document without offering an alternative verification method, effectively trapping the citizen.

Applying a new financial penalty or tax retroactively to a period before the law existed is widely regarded as fundamentally unjust (ex post facto) because the citizen had no notice or ability to comply at the time. A reasonable person would immediately agree that you cannot fairly penalize conduct that was permitted when it occurred.

The enforcement pattern is obviously unjust because the user is being targeted for minor infractions (shed near line) while an adjacent, more severe violation (garage crossing line) is ignored. A reasonable person would view the weekly inspections and repeated citations for the user, contrasted with total immunity for the neighbor's worse violation, as clear evidence of a vendetta or arbitrary application of the law.

The injustice is obvious because the authority fails to provide a knowable standard for compliance. A reasonable person would agree that it is unfair for a government body to enforce a rule while refusing to define it in writing and providing contradictory verbal instructions, making compliance a guessing game rather than a lawful process.

The case presents a clear instance of selective enforcement where the user is singled out for punishment while identical violations by twelve others are ignored in plain sight. A reasonable person would immediately recognize the unfairness of police walking past other unpermitted vendors to target one specific individual without justification.

The application of the rule is logically absurd and impossible to comply with. No reasonable person would defend a bureaucracy demanding documents that the government itself knows were destroyed, especially when the refusal to waive the requirement prevents essential safety repairs.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that refusing to verify a meter reading of 5,000 gallons/day—especially after the user proved the meter runs even with the water shut off—is unjust. The utility's dogmatic refusal to send a technician or provide an appeal mechanism constitutes a clear failure of due process in application.

Implementing a ban on accommodation effective immediately with zero grace period for bookings starting in 24 hours is procedurally unfair. A reasonable person would agree that trapping a host and stranding international travelers without notice constitutes an unjust application of regulatory power.

The injustice is obvious because the enforcement is driven by personal animus (former brother-in-law) rather than impartial administration of the law. A reasonable person would immediately condemn a situation where one resident is fined weekly while immediate neighbors with identical or more severe violations are ignored solely due to the enforcer's personal vendetta.
